quote_of_day.text: Having a sacred animal is the most retarded thing a culture could do. Eating beef is based, it's just a fucking animal. If you think it's a god get your malnutritioned brain checked. >muh milk Every other mammal gives milk, why is cow special.
quote_of_day.name: Savarkar-pilled
quote_of_day.topic: critique of sacred animals, dietary choices

guest_letters.title: Based website
guest_letters.body: Why aren’t we talking more about this IAC guy?? I just woke uop and checked bahut interesting site hai, there’s quotes and funny articles and board status?? We need more anons like iac to make cool shit. Im a very impressed pajeet. Writing this to see if it will get featured on the newspaper or not, im not sure how he set it up but if the !thing works then hello indus news! Im on da news niggaaaaaa
guest_letters.name: Anon

articles.title: Weather for Indus today
articles.body:
A low-pressure system of beef-eating controversy moved into the board, bringing with it squalls of religious debate and cultural critique.

The system's center made a blunt statement, calling sacred animals 'retarded' and sparking a heated response.

A scattered high-pressure ridge of relationship curiosity formed over the board, with an anon seeking advice on dating a girl from a different cultural background.

The discussion was marked by a gentle drizzle of questions about social compatibility and past relationships.

Meanwhile, a brief but intense fog of speculation rolled in around an upcoming girl group, with posters tossing around names and song lyrics.

A music thread also emerged, marked by a clear and calm atmosphere, with users sharing clean links to their favorite tracks.

However, a cold snap of aggression blew into a thread where an anon praised Dyaush, with replies quickly turning hostile.

articles.title: Eating Beef Sparks Debate on Faith and Culture
articles.body:
Savarkar-pilled initiated a discussion on the cultural significance of eating beef, calling it "based" and criticizing the notion of a sacred animal as "retarded." The original post sparked a heated debate among users, with many weighing in on the relationship between faith, culture, and dietary choices.

Dyaush responded by arguing that the issue is complex and influenced by one's framework and moral layer. They suggested that even if one doesn't care about animal welfare, the spiritual layer of their faith is a separate consideration.

Dyaush also referenced Savarkar's remarks on the importance of not being inbred and promoting a strong, non-degenerate population. The conversation took a turn when Dyaush explained that cows are not considered gods, but rather a protected entity in their culture.

They clarified that only some cows have the status of Aghniya, and that the idea of protecting them is not about worshipping them as divine beings. This explanation sparked further discussion on the nuances of faith and cultural practices.

Users also engaged in a side discussion on genetics, with Dyaush debunking the myth that the Y chromosome is weaker than the X. They explained that the Y chromosome has evolved to prevent impurity and is not self-governing like the X chromosome.

This exchange highlighted the diversity of topics and perspectives on the board. As the discussion progressed, Dyaush expressed frustration with users who were disrupting the conversation, calling them "retards" and "nigger faggots." They emphasized the importance of staying on topic and avoiding inflammatory comments.

Despite these challenges, the conversation continued, with users exploring topics such as dietary laws, faith, and cultural identity. One user proposed creating a cooking thread focused on a meat and seafood-heavy diet inspired by the Indus Valley Diet.

Dyaush responded positively, suggesting that such a thread could generate significant engagement, especially if it included buffalo meat. This proposal highlighted the ongoing interest in exploring cultural heritage and dietary practices.

The debate ultimately underscored the complexities of faith, culture, and personal choice, with users presenting diverse perspectives on these issues. The conversation reflected the nuances and challenges of navigating cultural and spiritual identities in a modern context.

articles.title: Anon Seeks Advice on Dating a UC Girl
articles.body:
Anon opened a discussion on dating a girl from a higher social standing. He mentioned being non-Brahmin but upper caste (UC), and having common interests with a KP girl whose ex-partner was a Mallu Brahmin.

Anon observed that the girl shows interest through eye contact but doesn't agree to casual meetups like grabbing food or watching a movie. Another anon chimed in, suggesting that mixed individuals may further mix due to lacking a coherent identity.

They cited the example of Kayasthas, who originated from Savarna Shudra but developed their distinct subculture over time. A respondent argued that the Kayasthas' history is more complex, with some converting to Buddhism to be considered upper caste.

They also mentioned that some Kayasthas look good, while others don't. Anon added that the kind of family one comes from plays a significant role, with liberal UC women being more open to marrying outside their caste.

Some anons discussed the differences between traditional and liberal upper castes, with the former being more inclined to marry within their Varna. One respondent stated that they would prefer not to marry outside their Varna if they can't find someone within it.

The conversation also touched on genetics and eugenics, with an anon advocating for aggressive eugenics among fair-skinned, high-IQ North Indian groups.

Another respondent, Dyaush, argued against the concept of AASI (Ancient Ancestral South Eurasian) being a single entity, calling it a representation of genetic drift. Anons shared their views on inter-caste marriages, with some supporting it and others preferring to marry within their Varna.

The discussion also involved comparisons between different castes and their marriage practices.

articles.title: Anon Speculates on Upcoming Girl Group
articles.body:
Anon initiated a discussion on an upcoming girl group called 'gobarjeeta kumari'.

The original poster wondered if the reader's sister was auditioning and expressed curiosity about the group's potential success, questioning whether it would fail like DEL48 or if Koreans were better than Japanese at creating international branches.

Anon responded with a seemingly unrelated anecdote about a 'momo wali pyaari /nep/yaari didi' who sold momos with her mother when he was a child.

He expressed fond memories of her and noted that he was happy to see a group that didn't hate East Asian appearances.

The conversation took a turn as another anon fantasized about secretly dating and having sex with an idol, citing it as a peak achievement for any man.

This prompted another anon to make crude comments about a Vtuber named Rushia, suggesting that she needed to be 'railed heavily'.

Anon also joked about the group's potential lineup, with one expressing hope that it wouldn't feature an unattractive 'gobarjeeta' like a certain individual.

Another anon sarcastically suggested that making a 15-16 year old an idol and having her perform in skimpy clothes in a country full of 'sexually frustrated unkills' would be a recipe for disaster.

The discussion also touched on the group's nationality, with one anon stating that it would be an all-Indian group but expressing hope for some variety in the lineup.

articles.title: Music Thread on /b/
articles.body:
Anon started a music thread, posting a link to J.J. Cale's 'Durango (rare)' and asking others to share tracks with clean links. Anon replied with a link to Mogwai's 'Auto Rock'.

Another anon shared A Perfect Circle's 'Disillusioned', calling it a great song about the effects of perpetually connected digital devices.

Anon also posted links to The War on Drugs' 'Thinking of a Place', Tame Impala's 'The Less I Know The Better', and Alan Parsons Project's 'Sirius + Eye In The Sky'.

articles.title: Anon Praises Dyaush, Replies Get Aggressive
articles.body: Anon expressed appreciation for Dyaush's actions on the board. Anon responded with mocking questions about Dyaush's ability to claim multiple castes at once. Replies quickly turned aggressive, with one anon calling others "low castes larping as high caste" and another making an obscene remark about the OP's mother. Dyaush responded with a cryptic message. The conversation devolved into name-calling and insults, with multiple anons hurling caste-based slurs.
